ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for cereal mixed with dried fruits and nuts; 'breakfast cereal food product' (FOODON_03301710) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000E covers breakfast cereals broadly; no specific sub-code exists for cereal with dried fruits and nuts in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for this mixed cereal product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is approximately 45g (about one small bowl) of dry cereal with fruits or nuts, often consumed with milk or yogurt at breakfast.

nutrient_source.title: Cereal with fruits or nuts — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Cereal with fruits or nuts' (CR ID: F05050006). All per-100g values are taken as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI value exists for 'cereal with fruits or nuts' as a composite product in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 66 is estimated based on the closest analogous entry: muesli (toasted, with fruit and nuts) reported at GI ~66 (glucose reference) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (66 × 37.06g) / 100 ≈ 24.4. Available carbohydrate per serving = 82.35g/100g × 45g serving = 37.06g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (28.57g/100g) exceeds the 10g/100g solid-food gate; sodium at 319mg/100g is borderline elevated; potassium at 44mg/100g is very low, providing no meaningful sodium offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this product due to its high sugar content and elevated sodium with negligible potassium; choose unsweetened muesli or plain oats with fresh fruit and a potassium-rich milk alternative instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 28.57g/100g far exceeds the 20g/100g diabetes hard gate; total carbohydrate is 82.35g/100g with a medium-high GI (~66), producing a high glycemic load per serving (~24.4) that will cause significant postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This cereal is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high sugar and total carbohydrate content; replace with plain rolled oats, unsweetened muesli, or a low-GI high-fibre cereal with fresh fruit and unsweetened milk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar (28.57g/100g) raises triglycerides and contributes to atherogenic dyslipidaemia; sodium at 319mg/100g is borderline; fat is relatively low (5.09g/100g) and cholesterol is negligible (0.2mg/100g), which are positive factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this product due to its high sugar content, which promotes hypertriglyceridaemia; if consumed, restrict portion to 30g and pair with unsweetened low-fat milk, choosing a lower-sugar cereal variant as a long-term substitute.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar (28.57g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA pathology, exceeding the RA hard gate for refined sugar >15g/100g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this cereal due to its high sugar content driving systemic inflammation; replace with plain oats topped with fresh walnuts and berries for anti-inflammatory benefit without the sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cereal with fruits or nuts is a processed breakfast cereal product consisting of grain-based cereal mixed with dried fruits (such as raisins, cranberries, or apricots) and nuts (such as almonds, cashews, or walnuts). It is typically consumed as a ready-to-eat or quick-preparation breakfast dish, served dry or with milk, yogurt, or a plant-based milk alternative. The texture is crunchy to chewy depending on the cereal base and the proportion of dried fruit, with a sweet flavour profile driven by the concentrated sugars in the dried fruit component and any added sweeteners. Per 100g, this product provides 402 kcal, 6.85g protein, 5.09g fat, 82.35g carbohydrate (of which 28.57g is sugar), 4.73g dietary fibre, and 319mg sodium. The high sugar content (28.57g/100g) makes this product unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, and cardiovascular disease. The dietary fibre content (4.73g/100g) provides some benefit for gut health and satiety, and the iron content (7.83mg/100g) is notably high, likely due to fortification. Calcium at 115mg/100g also suggests fortification. This food is classified as a processed grain-based dish and is commonly consumed at breakfast or as a snack in Singapore and internationally.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F05050006) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (muesli with fruit and nuts, University of Sydney GI Database) and is clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite cereal product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F05050006 as of 2026. Cholesterol is reported as 0.2mg/100g (trace level from nut component). GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on muesli with fruit and nuts from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is an internationally generic processed cereal product, so no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. The food is suitable for vegetarians and vegans (as a dry product); halal status is assumed based on plant-based ingredients but may vary by brand. Gluten-free status is not confirmed as the cereal base likely contains wheat or oats.
